comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

The illustration? One of the two famous drawings from the Aurea Catena Homeri (Golden Chain of Homer) by Anton Josef Kirchweger, one of the last of the red-hot central European alchemists. The two dragons represent the two principles of his alchemy, niter and salr (which of course are not the material substances called by these names) and the metals are shown around a hexagram in the middle. 

[IMAGE:1]

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:2]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain other gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I now have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it! 

***Brief Update: er, folks, this is Magic Monday, it's not Ask-Me-Anything Monday.  I've had to delete a bunch of questions on things unrelated to occultism. I do an open post on the fourth Wednesday of each month over on my blog for general questions. Thank you for keeping the conversation focused!***

***This Magic Monday is now closed. See you next week!***

I work with the Ogham fews, and lately I've been getting some confused readings. A couple of days ago, for example, I drew reversed Mor, reversed Saille with reversed Straif as the outcome. I took that to mean that some past disruptions will be an influence through the day, which may have to do with the subconscious and which I will have trouble dealing with (reverse Saille), resulting in great difficulty.

It actually ended up being a good day. A visit to a friend ended up being a bit unpleasant due to fatigue, so I suppose that could have been it. I also almost drove over a snake crossing the road, which seemed symbolic because snakes are showing up in my scryings and in my reading.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You're in the learning curve, and this sort of thing is normal. Most beginners overestimate how bad a bad reading is, and how good a good reading is; you've just learned, in the case of the reading you described, that you did this and assumed it would be an awful day when that's not what the fews were trying to tell you. This is normal. Adjust your interpretations accordingly and keep going.

(I don't read Straif reversed as "great difficulty", btw. It means that something difficult is going to happen and you can't avoid it, so you just need to grit your teeth and deal.)

comments.title: New Transcripts
comments.content:
I would like to announce 3 new transcripts


Tantra: Power of a Goddess - No. 4 (of 6)
https://happypanda.dreamwidth.org/#entry-6725

Tantra - Bring Your Imagination to Life - No. 5 (of 6)
https://happypanda.dreamwidth.org/#entry-6952

Are Isha Practices Tantric - 6 (of 6)
https://happypanda.dreamwidth.org/#entry-7226

I would like to say that although the questioner in the 3rd transcript asked about Isha the answers were not really about Isha but rather what exactly qualifies as a Tantra and what is a Yoga.

The interesting thing is that the 3rd transcript gives very specific criteria for what qualifies as a Tantra (psst. Levy's book fits all of them from what I can tell!)

After the 3rd transcript I gave 2 separate commentary but assuming JMG will permit it I'll C&P the first of the two here as I think some MM readers might information useful.

Also, as always - questions and comments are appreciated at my blog.

Cheers!

*****C&P of Commentary from 3rd Transcript*****


1st Commentary:

One of the key take-aways I got from this is


1. Tantra is procedural and uses things in one's environment to enhance well-being. [by Dharma tradition definitions - Western Magic definitely qualifies as a legitimate tantra and it's busy building and renewing its own authentic tantric lineages].


2. Tantra thus is a kind of technology.


3. It's governing principle is to use a procedure on living material (if you do A+B you get C) so as to unfold or reveal something which could not be done from the unfolding from that life on it's own.


4. Tantra as a procedural kind of "technology" - if you do it correctly long enough it will evolve into something completely unforeseen and unrecognisable to the practitioners. Once this happens the tantric procedure needs someone of greater wisdom and capabilities than the practitioners themselves to make sure the system doesn't twist into something that's abused and misused.


Now...putting all of the above together I noticed right away this process also describes the many-centuries long fascination of Europeans with the results of their machines and technology. It's as if Europeans wanted the benefits of Tantra (which needs living material to work on*) but wanted to see that same Tantric heightening beyond native capability and benefit happen with use of inert, non-living materials too! Heighten the possibilities of the inert in such a way that it could achieve things that would not be possible when just left to it's own nature. Which is the foundational premise of Tantra. Treat the inert, non-living with the same kind of proceduralness as Tantra does of living materials.

Thus...very weirdly...Tantra evolved into...materialist science! So it was no accident that Sir Isaac Newton was both an Alchemist Mage and a Scientist. And later it twisted again when it led to the Industrial Revolution. Thus, the "surprise tantric plot twist" led to the atheistic insistence that one doesn't need a god or unseen world to explain the world. It happens "mechanistically", "procedurally" and "evolutionarily". Yes, says Sadhguru because this is the very nature of anything Tantric. Over long enough time-frames when correctly done and maintained it will evolve into something completely unrecognisable.

Equally weirdly this in itself signifies something very Tantric-energy-wise was motivating Europeans as they were treating the inert so much the same as the living that they used Tantric proceduralness to unfold latent potentials in the Inert just like any practicing Tantric does of the living! You see something that appears different as the same. Scroll back in the transcript and read where Sadhguru says exactly this about Tantra. It finds the point in polarity that both poles share and sees the differences as the same.** This is EXACTLY what can make Tantra so powerful, potentially beneficial and when it all goes wrong - so debilitating and detrimental.


It was a hugely Tantric-way of seeing the Inert that was the unique gift Europeans brought to the world! Though, as Sadhguru has said - when it goes on long enough and heightens in pitch over time if someone of even greater wisdom than the inventors and users/practitioners doesn't (or can't) step in and guide it then it will invariably fall into misuse and abuse. Which is sadly exactly what has happened. (Sadhguru also had a talk about that too - see commentary #2 - which one can read if you click on the 3rd link to my blog)



This is an example of how a procedural technology used over a long-enough time period will give outcomes absolutely nobody anywhere can foresee. So now inert materials (ex: rare and common metals) give unforeseen results (radios, monitors, trains, jets, telephones, satellites, the internet, etc) one would never guess simply by leaving those inert components to continue in their own natures.


Sadhguru says because of the evolution of modern society’s digital high-tech there will come a twist once again in the far future when the very energy itself will encode information in a way no one on the planet will ever foresee. That means Star Trek, Star Wars, Dune, AI, whatever have not foreseen it either! And it will happen in a way that no one, no matter how high their IQ, will be able to guess.


[Final sub-comment: It is, in my opinion, very likely that this evolutionary "twist" Sadhguru talked about will occur long after humanity as a species no longer exists on this planet and it will be Earth's next intelligent species who will be left with having to deal with said "surprise twist" that humanity initiated inert, non-living materials into.]


This also shows why predicting what will happen in the future strictly going by what's going on today or what happened in the past is bound to fail. Tantra by it's nature will evolve it's own procedure into something completely unforeseen and this is native to Tantric process. So what is true for the individual is doubly true for society as a whole. Humanity's use of Tantric-processes will evolve into something unforeseen and unless great wisdom and sense of responsibility, restraint and clarity comes then it will end up being abused and misused. Which - as one can see from my 2nd commentary - is precisely what has happened to humanity today.



*yes, I know Tantra and spiritual processes worldwide has always used inert materials in ceremony and ritual but it has never used inert material as the foundational material for the Inert itself to be the thing heightened via the ceremony or ritual procedure. It was European culture uniquely that seemed to stumble upon applying that Tantric procedure of heightening living matter to apply it to the Inert itself that delivered the 'tantric surprise plot twist' Sadhguru says is always the hallmark of any Tantric process that successfully goes on long enough.


**It just occurred to me this is what a lot of mathematicians attempt to do too within their discipline.

Hi JMG and commentariat!

Over on conjunction.dreamwidth.org, I posted a series of geomantic readings on the topic, “Will the House Burn Down This Year?” This is a reasonable question, since it is wildfire season here in British Columbia, we have had record high temperatures, and there are currently a lot of wildfires. The answer to my query seems to be ‘yes.’
(Mothers: Via, Laetitia, Laetitia, Fortuna Minor. See post for discussion comments.)

Question 1 – Nature of Perfection (a ‘yes’ answer) in a Geomantic Chart;
Is a ‘yes’ answer in a Geomantic Chart a firm yes, ie., “yes, no matter what you do, it is gonna happen,”
Or possibly does it provide critically important information, a likely outcome, but not necessarily certainty? This would be along the lines of Astrology’s, “The stars incline but do not compel.”

Naturally I want to know when the house (will? Is likely to?) burn down.
I cast a shield chart first, using Witness 2 as Quesited, decided I should have cast with a house chart in mind, and so re-cast a house chart with House IV as Quesited. Other commenters made a good case for using House VIII as the Quesited. Looking at both charts and collating data for IV and VIII, I get two groups of results; July 9 to 20, and Sept 18 to 24.

Question 2 – Geomantic Time (APG pp. 147-150).
Is the House Chart a better choice for time readings, or could a Shield Chart be used, as above?

Question 3 -- For this particular question, would you have used House IV, House VIII, or something else as the Quesited?

Unlike the people living in the shadow of the dam, I am doing everything I can to prevent the house from burning down, and will continue my efforts no matter what feedback I get on the topic. If nothing else, Spiritus Mundi has made me highly motivated to fix the landscaping and other features that put my house at risk for catching fire. That can’t be a bad thing.

The results are verifiable (!) so to me, it seems to be an excellent case for learning about Geomancy, no matter how things turn out. Thanks for any insights anyone has on this one.

--Lavender Crunchy Snail

JMG, I was the one who argued the 8th house would be more pertinent to this question. Now, to be clear, it doesn’t affect the outcome of EmmanuelG’s initial reading, which clearly perfects whether by 4th or by 8th (though the aspects are kinder to him in the 8th version).

My argument for the 8th had nothing to do with real estate. Having done daily geomancy readings for over a year now, the 8th house only rarely seems to have anything to do with death, magic, or other people’s money (2nd of the 7th). Well, “death” works, but only in the sense of being the ultimate limit. The 8th is more generally the house of limits and loss, which balances the 2nd house’s identity as the house of possession and gain. I believe its traditional relationship to magic and the spirit world come not only from the connection with death, but also from— maybe even primarily from— its meaning of limitations, as magic is the way of willingly guiding the forces of the universe toward particular outcomes rather than just anywhere they would have gone through natural momentum. But for most questions, I find that what appears in the 8th doesn’t have to do with magic, spirits, or death per se, so much as things which are being limited or forced to end in some way.

It would be silly for me to argue that EmmanuelG’s question wasn’t about real estate— and of course it was. What I was suggesting, though, was that the real heart of the question, as it was about loss and crisis, might more properly be considered an 8th house question.

Regardless of your own judgment as to where to assign EmmanuelG’s question. Does my understanding of the 8th house accord with yours, and if not, where in your view should I revise or reconsider?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There are many definitions of magic. I use that one because it fools people into thinking that it means they can fit magic into the materialist worldview. (I'm pretty sure Fortune had the same thing in mind when she coined it.) To begin with, the definition doesn't specify whose consciousness is being changed, or how, or whether those changes are being carried out entirely within the scientific worldview.

If you go a little deeper you'll discover that in occult philosophy, consciousness is not just a property of lumps of meat called human brains -- it's a continuum, a substance filling the cosmos, which is why thoughts can pass from mind to mind and things we think of as "dead matter" actually possess consciousness and life in forms that materialists simply can't recognize.

Finally, at a deeper level still, you'll find that consciousness is the substance from which all things are made. Our bodies are a modality of consciousness. So is the chair you're sitting on and the air that you breathe. "All things are mind," says the Kybalion -- that's what it's trying to communicate. So if you can change consciousness in accordance with will, what can you not change?

But it's very difficult for people raised to believe in materialist codswallop to fit their heads around this, so a definition that can be understood by them at their current level, and deepens as (and if) they do, is useful.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I can speak to my own experience of “falling” for magic because of this definition, and my own take on the philosophical implications

I still consider myself fairly rooted in materialist thought, but moving from thinking of it as “reality” to “one of the ways to interpret this unknowable process we call reality.”

I am extremely interested in the philosophical implications of quantum mechanics. I used to “believe” in the many-worlds interpretation: everything that can exist will exist, the question of free will is meaningless (if there’s a world for every possible choice I am ever going to make, what does it even mean to have or not have free will?)

However, if will is fundamental to consciousness, this interpretation falls apart. If you start from this assumption, will exists because I’m conscious. A bit further along this thought process I realize things like “my choices are who i am.” Going back to quantum physics, I’m drawn to the conclusion that the “choices” made by the universe (anything from an electron going through a double slit to my choice to have a sandwich) matter, and there can be only one timeline.

Hope that at least leads to some fruitful meditation for you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Quantum mechanics, and many worlds, is very interesting, and there's no saying that such theories don't more or less accurately depict how things unfold on the material plane. It's just that the material plane is one aspect of the greater cosmos. Crucially, the observer problem in quantum mechanics, if read straight up, leads to the disturbing (to materialists) implication that the observer (a consciousness) is required for there to be anything at all.

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

comments.title: Protestant Middle Pillar
comments.content: Would you be willing to create and share a variant of the Middle Pillar ritual that would go with the Christian Banishing rite mentioned on this blog? Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Protestant Middle Pillar
replies.content:
I've posted one more than once. Here it is:

First, perform the complete Banishing Ritual of the Cross.

Second, imagine a ray of light descending from infinite space to form a sphere of light just above your head, around eight inches in diameter. This sphere is white and contains a blood-red cross. When you have formulated it clearly in your imagination, vibrate the word IESOUS three times. (This is the Greek version of Jesus. Give each vowel its full pronounciation: "ee-ay-so-oos.")

Third, bring the ray of light to your throat, and form another sphere. This one is violet and contains a blood-red cross. When you have formulated it, vibrate the word CHRISTOS three times -- "kh-ree-s-toh-s."

Fourth, bring the ray of light to your heart, and form a third sphere, which is brilliant gold like the sun, and contains a blood-red cross. The word THEIOS is vibrated here three times; again, pronounce each vowel -- "thay-ee-oss."

Fifth, bring the ray of light to your genital center, and form a fourth sphere. This one is green and contains a blood-red cross. HUIOS is vibrated here three times -- "hoo-ee-oss."

Sixth, bring the ray of light to your feet, and form a fifth center. This one is black and contains a blood-red cross. The name vibrated here, three times, is SOTER, "soh-tear."

(The words, of course, are Greek for "Jesus Christ, Son of God, Savior," and their initials spell out the Greek word for "fish." That sequence was used by Christians to identify themselves to each other in the earliest days of the church, and it identifies the practitioner with the Christians of those days. The five red crosses symbolize the five wounds Christ suffered in the crucifixion.)

Seventh, return the attention to the center at the top of the head. Bring a current of white light a few inches wide down the left side of the head and neck, the left shoulder and arm, and theleft hip and leg, down to the center at the feet, and then back up the right leg and hip, the right arm and shoulder, and the right side of the neck and head, back to the center at the head. Repeat this a total of four times; if possible, synchronize with the breath, so that the energy flows down with the outbreath and up with the inbreath, but it is more important to visualize the whole course than to make it happen within a single breath.

Eighth, in the same way, bring a current of white light from the center above the head, down the midline of the front of the body to the center at the feet, and then back up the midline of the back of the body to the center above the head. Repeat a total of four times.

Ninth, turn the attention to the center at the feet. Breathing in, draw a current of energy up the Middle Pillar of the body from that center to the center above the head; breathing out, allow it to spray like a fountain out and over the whole body, cleansing the entire aura, pooling at the feet and being drawn back up the Middle Pillar with the next inbreath. Do this a total of four times.

Tenth, make the sign of the cross with the usual words. This completes the exercise.

comments.title: Karmic ponderings
comments.content:
I know it isn't necessarily wise to speculate on the karma of others, but am I correct in thinking that what you do not do can incur negative karma just as much as positive, especially if you are the one who set it in motion?

For context - let's assume that I am the founder of a company which starts small,but grows remarkably quickly and ends up with many people working for me. (Though for clarity, I am not) Due to some initial mis-steps around setting the company culture, it effectively just carries on, and many employees are mis-treated. I assume that doing nothing would incur negative karma to both yourself, for initiating it, and to those actually responsible for carrying it out? Assuming you realised and wanted to fix it, how would be best to deal with it in that instance - to try to improve the company culture (possibly by firing the lackeys who were 'only following orders') or to publicly shame the company, disavow all association with it, and walk away to let it collapse on its own?

Equally, taken a step further, if I have some savings set aside, but choose not to donate any of it to charity, does that incur karma one way or another?

Also, does planetary charity still grant positive karma, as you are effectively doing it partially to benefit yourself?

Karma is a subject I find very confusing the more you drill into the details. You've mention the Lords of Karma before as well - what are they (eg. A class of angel, spirit, etc?), or is it effectively just a name for a natural force as impersonal as gravity or friction? (if we assume there are not classes of spirit responsible for these!) If the former, can they be contacted to enquire on the karma of doing X or Y?

replies.title: Re: Karmic ponderings
replies.content: Karma is complicated in practice, though it's simple in essence. The basic rule is that whatever you do shapes who you are, and thus shapes what you'll experience in the future of this life and future lives. Choosing not to do something is itself an action, and makes you the kind of person that other people won't bother to help.  If you walk away from the mess you created, unless you made a serious attempt to fix it and walked away only when that failed, you can expect to have other people's messes left for you to fix. "What you do is what will be done to you" is a rough approximation, but it works quite a bit more often than not. As for the Lords of Karma, I've always thought of it as a metaphor for an impersonal process.

replies.title: Re: Karmic ponderings
replies.content:
That clears that up nicely, thank you!

I don't envy the karma of a lot of today's CEOs in that case though...

replies.title: Re: Karmic ponderings
replies.content: Neither do I. They can expect to spend a lot of lives in which other people profit off them and don't care if they live or die.

replies.title: Re: Karmic ponderings
replies.content: It's not exactly a new observation that plenty of people seem to get away with morally questionable behavior, at least in their present incarnation. Why is there such a delay between the accumulation of karma and the consequences? Is the universe "imperfect" in that sense?

replies.title: Re: Karmic ponderings
replies.content: The universe, or at least this part of it, is set up to facilitate certain kinds of learning. Letting people go to extremes within the boundaries of one life seems to be part of that.

comments.title: 2 events during ritual practice
comments.content:
Dear JMG, I hope all is well, and first I want to take the opportunity to thank you for the maintenance of this public good.

Its been now 2 weeks since adding the middle pillar exercise to my ritual routine, and it has been quite rewarding. Aside from feeling disciplined and motivated, I can clearly "feel" things, that go from random tingles, to feelings of excitement, arousal, and definitely a new found receptiveness when meditating.

But two things stood out (in two different days), which I would like to explain in detail.

1)
I started my usual practice before going to bed - which is a candle excersise/medidation - I start by practicing conscious gratitude (so a big thank you for everything prayer)whilst focusing on the candle, I do this whilst sitting in front of a small altar to thoth which has a ceramic baboon and an alabaster Ibis - frankenincense burning, then meditate. I then proceed to read a chapter/excerpt of a book. This time it was Eliphas Levi's The doctrine and ritual of high magic (chapter 2). The reading (and rituals that come afterwards) I dedicate to thoth, and ask for wisdom and guidance. Anyway as I was in my reading - a big fly flew directly into the flame of the (thin) white candle. It got caught in the wax (candle basically had a diameter of a little bit more than the fly itself). It stuck there and burnt and died almost immeadeately. I thought of stopping and restarting - but I actually continued reading .. wondering of it meant something. The way it happened it certainly felt like it did. I know flies are attracted to light, but I also know of coincidences and synchronicities as possible means of communication. In many ways it felt like something beyond me.. so I proceeded with my ritual (Qabalistic cross, LBRP, Middle pillar, QC) - and then some quiet meditation.

Any ideas if there is a possible symbolism related to flies/fire or just a voluntary self sacrifice of a living being in the middle of your work?



Second, (and more recently). As I finished the same routine as aboive, and after my full ritual (QC, LBRP, MP, QC), and did my final meditation in front of the candle. This meditation comes with a lot of sensory feelings that come from the ritual, but also, a great easiness into a calmness which I much enjoy. As I let go into this calmness, I started noticing a purple light, bringht center, and I started to focus on it quite intently. It increased in size, pulsating, becoming very visible.
Yesterday I happened again, specifically again in the end meditation. Although in yesterday's, the purple pulsating light (and small aura) expanded to a bigger aura, which by the end changed to a more bluish hue. This happened after I asked if there was something there, or what this meant.

After some time and no response, I open my eyes, blow the candle saying "the flame, not the light", and leave to bed.

So, all in all, these are the two events I wanted to share. The fly, and the purple light, pone internal, and one external.. altho that is quite the arbitrary distinction I am making there. Would love to hear your thoughts

replies.title: Re: 2 events during ritual practice
replies.content: These things happen. Their meaning is rarely apparent at the time. Note them down in your practice journal, and consider meditating on them as symbols. What does a fly symbolize to you? What does the color purple mean to you?  Let these things unfold their meanings that way.

comments.title: “breaking the spirit” of a child
comments.content: Over the years I have occasionally heard of teachers or parents talk about the old-fashioned idea of “breaking the will” or “breaking the spirit” of a child. I always thought this was a figure of speech because I reasoned that if one were really able to succeed in destroying a human being’s will, it would be a form of abuse, or even torture. One’s will is so basic to one’s whole identity.  I understand magic deals with the will of a person, so I wonder: What is the magical perspective on this practice? Is such a thing even possible, and if so, is it child abuse?

replies.title: Re: “breaking the spirit” of a child
replies.content:
Yes, breaking a child's spirit was an actual thing as recently as the first half of the 20th century, and it was not at all rare.

My father's mother deliberately tried to break my father's spirit when he was a boy. He was her third and last child, and her much-loved husband died when he was about 4 months old, about 6 years into their marriage. So she renamed my father after her lost husband and refused to let him go to school, or even to wear "boy" clothes instead of the dresses that babies of both sexes were routinely clothed with, until he was about 8 years old. Eventually his somewhat older brother intervened and persuaded their mother to let him grow up. So he was allowed to act his age, and a few years later, when she told him to do something, he refused -- he "defied her," as she put it when telling the story. She advanced on him to force his obedience, and he picked up a stick to defend himself. As grandmother told the story, she was frightened by that, but she believed she would "lose him" if she let him defy her, so she overcame her fear of being hit, took his stick away, and "gave him the beating of his life to break his spirit." After that, she said, he never dared defy her again. What she never said aloud, but was obvious to me, was that he never loved her after that either. I think grandmother even knew it, but didn't much care about love one way or another. She told this story about breaking her son's spirit more than once, and she always told it proudly, as one of her triumphs in her life.

I should add two footnotes to this story. First, grandmother's own father was quite a monster, and her parents' marriage was a nightmare of mutual aggression and reciprocal violence throughout all its 50 years.

And second, my own father was wise enough and determined enough to break the pattern of abuse that he could have inherited. I couldn't have asked for a kinder, more understanding and loving parent.

In point of fact, child abuse was extremely common, and it was generally accepted by everyone (even by some of its victims) as just another fact of life up until about 1950 or even 1960: children were not commonly viewed as people with rights of their own, but as almost the property of their fathers, to do with as they pleased. (In fact, until the late 19th or early 20th century, wives, too, were commonly viewed as the property of their husbands.) This was simply the world I grew up in, back in the 1940s and early 1950s. The changes since then have been enormous, but I don't think more recent generations know just how very bad things were for children (and for women) back then.

replies.title: Re: “breaking the spirit” of a child
replies.content: What Robert said. Yes, it happens, and yes, it's child abuse.

replies.title: Re: “breaking the spirit” of a child
replies.content: Thanks both. It’s inspiring what your father did, Robert.

replies.title: Re: “breaking the spirit” of a child
replies.content: You're welcome, and thank you in turn!  It took a lot of something quite rare to do what he did.  He was never a happy man, and sometimes he would say that he wished he had never been born at all, so hard did he find life to live.  But he was a thoughtful man and a brave man.  It was he who triumphed in the end.  He died in 1970, before he turned 60, his health ruined by all the stress and struggle of his life.

replies.title: Re: “breaking the spirit” of a child
replies.content:
Robert, are prayers for your poor father okay?

My parents both suffered what today would be called child abuse. Grandma once threw Mom through a glass door; Mom was twelve-ish at the time. Dad was what is today called an Adult Child of an Alcoholic—Grandpa was an alcoholic and they lived way out in the backwoods, where there was no one to help even if doing so would have occurred to anyone at the time. So my parents both had what is today recognized as PTSD but could get no help.

replies.title: Re: “breaking the spirit” of a child
replies.content:
I'm sorry for your parents' suffering, Lady Cutekitten. Such stories are so common ... alas!

My father might even welcome your prayers for him, so thank you on both our behalfs. But, so far as I can tell, he never thought of himself as "poor"; most people's lives, he thought, were pretty horrible from birth to death, and you just had to get on with the duties of life anyway. He said more than once that he wished he could believe in something beyond this world, but never could find any good reason to hold even a glimmer of hope that there might be; and he certainly wasn't going to do the "wishful thinking" thing.

He did know that I loved and admired him, which comforted him at the end. I had time before he died to write him the most heart-felt letter I have ever written anyone, and my mother told me that he read and reread it a lot in the days before he died. (We were on oppposite sides of the continent, and circumstances utterly kept me from traveling to see him during his last months.)

He was named James Christian Mathiesen, after his deceased father.

(I should mention that in Denmark, Christian is simply one of the most common given names for boys. About half of the Kings of Denmark in recent centuries have been named Christian; most of the rest were named Frederik. replies.title: Re: LRM reading assignment
replies.content:
That's Butler's personal reworking of the Middle Pillar exercise. I know a few people who do it that way, but it's not something I recommend at all, for the reasons you've mentioned. It's also counterproductive to break the magical connection with the planetary spirit of the Earth, because that connection is an important stabilizing force.

There's another dimension as well. Planetary spirits don't have Divine Sparks the way we do; they come into being by a different process and evolve in a different way. It is through sharing in our experiences -- and the experiences of every other living thing on Earth -- that the planetary spirit of the Earth evolves and develops spiritually. Break the link, and sure, you may achieve your own advancement a little more quickly, but you're depriving the Earth of your share of her evolution. That seems very selfish to me.

replies.title: Re: Fasting
replies.content:
If I may, with regard to 1: I can't offer much on the esoteric side, but hopefully some of this is useful.

A few years ago I tried out the ketogenic diet and paired that with some fasting periods (especially to get back into ketosis after eating foods that took me out of it). If you aren't aware, "ketosis" is a state where your body begins breaking down fat to produce "ketones", which it can use as energy in place of glucose - this is most important for your brain, which can't use normal fatty acids or proteins for energy, since they don't pass the blood/brain barrier (but ketones and glucose do).

So, the goal of juice-type fasts is to give you the minimum amount of glucose you need to not get groggy and cranky, and the goal of water fasts is to get into ketosis. Most of my experience is with water/minimal calorie fasts that avoid carbohydrates, so that's reflected below.

Anyhow, some practical tips based on this: the first and biggest is to consider adding some salt and/or other electrolytes to your water while fasting to avoid headaches, cramping, and lower back pain. Magnesium seems to be especially helpful for this. If you are willing to be a little less strict with rules on what to drink, having a single cup of salted broth will help keep you feeling much better on the first day, but might trigger a bit of hunger cravings. Even just a pinch of regular salt per 16 or 32 oz or so will help a lot in not feeling miserable (unless, of course, physical suffering is one of the points).

For getting into a fast, some ways to make it easier, based off some advice I read/heard by Tim Ferriss:

1) the night before, have an early-ish dinner. If ketosis is your goal, have a high-fat, lower carbohydrate meal. Steak and green veggies with some butter is an excellent choice. Sleep in a bit later the next day, if you can. This way, you're asleep for much of the initial "hey, where's my glucose?" your body goes through.
2) If you can, go for a long walk - any light to moderate exercise is good, but walking seems to be especially helpful, which makes sense when you realize that not having enough food to eat and needing to walk long distances likely went together for much of human evolution. Avoid strenuous exercise, especially on the first day, as that will contribute to the headaches and cramping.

Depending on how comfortable you are with lab-made food products, another option is to buy "exogenous ketones". Basically, your body turns fat into ketones in the liver. Some companies turn fat into ketones in a factory and put it in bottles. This is immediately available to your body and brain as a source of energy, but doesn't interfere with the body's process of switching over to ketosis. In the past, I used these on the first day or two of the fast to help keep my energy up and my cravings down, but be careful - many folks have pretty strong reaction that one brand colorfully refers to as "disasterpants". Gastrointestinal distress is pretty common with any kind of major diet change, and switching from a heavy carbohydrate to a no or low-carbohydrate diet (even for a day or two) seems to have this effect on a lot of people - so be careful with that long walk!

For further discussion, Dom D'Agostino is a researcher whose lab first developed these, and Peter Attia is an MD with a focus on life-extension who has done extensive self-experimentation with the ketogenic diet. Neither have any kind of esoteric bent at all, and I haven't revisted them since getting into that side of things more seriously, but from everything I've heard and read about them, they're smart, decent people that practice what they recommend, so they might be some resources on the medical/biological side of fasting.

Lastly, as a bit of personal anecdote, as JMG has cautioned in his writings on diet, this is one of those areas with enormous variation from person to person. Some people report feeling shocking mental clarity when in a state of ketosis, high levels of energy, improved mood, basically everything's great. I never had that. I felt less fatigued, less grumpy when I was hungry, and otherwise okay, but not amazing. Later, I got a result back from a genetic test that said I might have a decreased ability to naturally create ketones - so much for universal wonder diets!

comments.title: Discursive meditation
comments.content:
Hullo JMG!

I hope you are doing well!

I've noticed throughout my journey through the CGD that I have difficulty with discursive meditation, as I'm sure is fairly normal, but I have some questions about it.

Sometimes I find it difficult to begin practicing, and kindve throw myself into to make sure it gets done so I don't think myself into a corner or procrastinate all my time away.

One consequence of this, is that on those days I often get to the meditation without any idea of what theme I want to meditate on.

And I end up simply sitting, and getting lost in trains of thought and imaginings, sometimes resolving that back towards a meditation theme, but sometimes I feel simply too tired to try and focus and want to let my mind run.

I assume that this is not adequately done discursive meditation.

Sometimes this letting my mind run, thoughts work themselves out and emotions process, is intensely relieving. Probably because I don't make enough time in my daily life to deal with all that already.

I think perhaps in writing this I am beginning to answer my own question.

This relaxing and letting the mind run seems to be an important process, but likely is not discursive meditation, and should be isolated from my daily CGD meditative practice.

What do you think of this conclusion?

Perhaps in order to get me to actually discursively meditate each day I should select a text like the Enchiridion and meditate through it line by line. (I've meditated at least once on all the directly recommend themes in the ovate grade so far, and returned through the major ones once as well)

I've also begun to simply be more aware of my mind and mental processes.

And I wonder, is complete control of my mind and thinking processes something I should strive for?

Is the ability to achieve mental silence whenever desired part of this, and is that possible?

How much effort do I need to put into this, without overdoing it?

If you have time, I would also appreciate your thoughts on these questions.

Thank you,

-WindMan

replies.title: Re: Discursive meditation
replies.content:
First of all, relax. Discursive meditation isn't easy, and it takes time and effort. The kind of difficulties you've had so far are quite common.

Second, complete control of your mind and thinking processes is not the goal of meditation, nor is mental silence. The goal is a process, not an endpoint -- an orientation toward becoming more aware of your own thoughts and subjective experiences, so that you can work with them and move them in more productive directions.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Counterpoint: While this comment does baldly state the facts of a particular scene in the show, it resolutely ignores some of the themes of the show, the primary one being that a faceless bureaucracy tries to rein in Loki, who then (spoilers) completely subverts them.

To put it another way: Do you think the kids (the target audience, remember) of this product went away with the impression that faceless bureaucrats who engage in struggle sessions are the "good guys"? I can tell you now what my kids impression was. And what do you think an entire generation of kids who watched this will conclude, on a grander scale?

For some perspective, do you think the kids who watched "Ghostbusters" concluded that the EPA was the "good guys"?

The other thematic point is that an element of chaos is integral to the universe, and to free will itself, and cannot be easily reigned in by the forces of order.

As I've posted elsewhere, I'm not here to argue this is high art (it's not something I personally enjoyed, FWIW). Nor should you or anyone be compelled to think the series, or any series, is "good," especially if you feel it dishonors your gods (believe me, I'm not lining up to watch an anime version of the Greco-Roman pantheon anytime soon). And whether these themes are "true" or whether they disrespect the gods is arguable.

But a lot of the hand-wringing over these movies seems to willfully ignore the (sometimes subversive) implications of the films, and what those wider effects will be on the generational consciousness. It also feels like the aghast reactions some must have had to the pulp fiction of the past, whether it was Lovecraft, Howard, and on down the line.

On another level: Do you think that on some level a divinity is not involved with propagating the images of gods through a medium that will impact the consciousnesses of millions of people around the world? Do you not think that some of these kids will further explore, in a devotional way, perhaps, the religions and gods associated with them?

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

replies.title: (no subject)

The Druid Path is for complete beginners. It's aimed at people who have no previous exposure to Druidry, nature spirituality, or occultism, and who want to dip their toes in the water a bit.
